---
title: "A/B Testing a Call to Action"
subtitle: "Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics"
author: "Fei Qin"
date: today
description: "A simulation-based walkthrough of LLN, bootstrap uncertainty, CLT, hypothesis testing, regression equivalence, and the peeking problem in A/B testing."
image: "ab_testing_thumbnail.png"
---

## Introduction

Suppose I run a website with a landing page where visitors can sign up for a newsletter. I want to know whether changing the call-to-action (CTA) text can increase sign-ups. I compare two versions:

- CTA A: "Sign up for our newsletter here!"
- CTA B: "Stay up to date by signing up!"

Visitors are randomly assigned to one CTA and produce a binary outcome: sign up (`1`) or not (`0`). This post uses that A/B test setup to demonstrate key tools from frequentist inference: large-sample behavior, uncertainty quantification, formal testing, and practical pitfalls.

## The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem

Let:

- \( \pi_A \): probability of sign-up under CTA A
- \( \pi_B \): probability of sign-up under CTA B
- \( \theta = \pi_A - \pi_B \): treatment effect (difference in conversion rates)

I estimate \( \theta \) with:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
where each sample mean is a sample proportion because outcomes are binary.

## Simulating Data

In a real experiment, \( \pi_A \) and \( \pi_B \) are unknown. In simulation, I choose them so I can compare estimates to ground truth. I set:

- \( \pi_A = 0.22 \)
- \( \pi_B = 0.18 \)
- true \( \theta = 0.04 \)

and simulate 1,000 visitors per group.

```{r}
set.seed(123)

n_A <- 1000
n_B <- 1000
pi_A <- 0.22
pi_B <- 0.18

signup_A <- rbinom(n_A, size = 1, prob = pi_A)
signup_B <- rbinom(n_B, size = 1, prob = pi_B)

pi_hat_A <- mean(signup_A)
pi_hat_B <- mean(signup_B)
theta_hat <- pi_hat_A - pi_hat_B

sim_summary <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Estimated sign-up rate (A)", "Estimated sign-up rate (B)", "Estimated difference (A - B)", "True difference"),
  value = c(pi_hat_A, pi_hat_B, theta_hat, pi_A - pi_B)
)

knitr::kable(sim_summary, digits = 3, caption = "Baseline simulation results")
```

The estimated difference is close to, but not exactly, 0.04. That gap is ordinary sampling variability.

## The Law of Large Numbers

The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) says sample averages stabilize around population values as \(n\) grows. For this A/B test, the running estimate of \( \hat{\theta} \) should settle near 0.04.

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=4.5}
diff_vector <- signup_A - signup_B
running_avg <- cumsum(diff_vector) / seq_along(diff_vector)

plot(
    seq_along(running_avg),
    running_avg,
    type = "l",
    lwd = 2,
    col = "#1B5E20",
    xlab = "Number of Observations",
    ylab = "Running estimate of theta",
    main = "Law of Large Numbers for the A/B Treatment Effect"
)

abline(h = 0.04, lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "#B71C1C")
legend("topright", legend = c("Running estimate", "True theta = 0.04"),
       col = c("#1B5E20", "#B71C1C"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```

Early values fluctuate, but the path stabilizes as data accumulate. That is exactly the LLN pattern.

## Bootstrap Standard Errors

Point estimates are incomplete without uncertainty. I estimate uncertainty in \( \hat{\theta} \) using a nonparametric bootstrap and compare it to the analytical standard error for two proportions.

```{r}
set.seed(123)

B <- 1000
bootstrap_theta <- numeric(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
    boot_A <- sample(signup_A, size = n_A, replace = TRUE)
    boot_B <- sample(signup_B, size = n_B, replace = TRUE)
    bootstrap_theta[b] <- mean(boot_A) - mean(boot_B)
}

boot_se <- sd(bootstrap_theta)

analytic_se <- sqrt(
  (pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A)) / n_A +
  (pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B)) / n_B
)

ci_lower <- theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper <- theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

uncertainty_tbl <- data.frame(
  quantity = c("Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE", "95% CI lower bound", "95% CI upper bound"),
  value = c(boot_se, analytic_se, ci_lower, ci_upper)
)

knitr::kable(uncertainty_tbl, digits = 4, caption = "Uncertainty estimates for theta")
```

The bootstrap and analytical SE are very close, which is reassuring. In this simulation, the 95% interval excludes 0, supporting a positive effect for CTA A.

## The Central Limit Theorem

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) implies that the sampling distribution of \( \hat{\theta} \) becomes approximately Normal for large \(n\), even with Bernoulli outcomes.

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=10, fig.height=7}
set.seed(123)

sample_sizes <- c(25, 50, 100, 500)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

for (n in sample_sizes) {
    theta_vals <- numeric(1000)

    for (i in 1:1000) {
    sim_A <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_A)
    sim_B <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = pi_B)
    theta_vals[i] <- mean(sim_A) - mean(sim_B)
    }

    hist(
    theta_vals,
    main = paste("n =", n),
    xlab = "Estimated treatment effect (theta_hat)",
    xlim = c(-0.3, 0.4),
    col = "#90CAF9",
    border = "white"
    )
    abline(v = pi_A - pi_B, lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "#B71C1C")
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

At small \(n\), the histograms are rough and dispersed. As \(n\) increases, they become tighter and more bell-shaped around the true effect.

## Hypothesis Testing

Using CLT-based normal approximation, I test:

- \(H_0: \theta = 0\)
- \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\)

```{r}
z_stat <- theta_hat / analytic_se
p_value <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_stat)))

hypothesis_tbl <- data.frame(
  statistic = c("z statistic", "two-sided p-value"),
  value = c(z_stat, p_value)
)

knitr::kable(hypothesis_tbl, digits = 4, caption = "Hypothesis test results")
```

The p-value is small, so I reject \(H_0\) at conventional levels. In this simulated sample, CTA A outperforms CTA B.

## The T-Test as a Regression

The two-sample difference-in-means is algebraically equivalent to the slope in:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
where \(D_i=1\) for CTA A and \(0\) for CTA B.

```{r}
ab_data <- data.frame(
    Y = c(signup_A, signup_B),
    D = c(rep(1, n_A), rep(0, n_B))
)

reg_fit <- lm(Y ~ D, data = ab_data)
reg_out <- as.data.frame(coef(summary(reg_fit)))
reg_out$term <- rownames(reg_out)
rownames(reg_out) <- NULL
reg_out <- reg_out[, c("term", "Estimate", "Std. Error", "t value", "Pr(>|t|)")]

knitr::kable(reg_out, digits = 4, caption = "Linear probability model (Y ~ D)")
```

The coefficient on `D` matches \( \hat{\theta} \). This regression framing is useful because it extends naturally to controls, covariates, and multiple treatment arms.

## The Problem with Peeking

Peeking means repeatedly checking significance before the planned sample size is reached and stopping early after the first "significant" result. This inflates false positives.

To show this, I simulate data under the null (\(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\)), test every 100 observations, and record whether any peek rejects at \(\alpha = 0.05\).

```{r}
set.seed(123)

num_experiments <- 10000
false_positive <- logical(num_experiments)
peek_points <- seq(100, 1000, by = 100)

for (j in 1:num_experiments) {
    A_null <- rbinom(1000, size = 1, prob = 0.20)
    B_null <- rbinom(1000, size = 1, prob = 0.20)

    peek_reject <- FALSE

    for (n in peek_points) {
    A_sub <- A_null[1:n]
    B_sub <- B_null[1:n]

    theta_sub <- mean(A_sub) - mean(B_sub)
    se_sub <- sqrt(
      (mean(A_sub) * (1 - mean(A_sub))) / n +
      (mean(B_sub) * (1 - mean(B_sub))) / n
    )

    if (se_sub > 0) {
        z_sub <- theta_sub / se_sub
      p_sub <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_sub)))

        if (p_sub < 0.05) {
        peek_reject <- TRUE
        break
        }
    }
    }

    false_positive[j] <- peek_reject
}

mean(false_positive)
```

```{r}
peeking_tbl <- data.frame(
  quantity = c("Nominal alpha", "Empirical false-positive rate under peeking"),
  value = c(0.05, mean(false_positive))
)

knitr::kable(peeking_tbl, digits = 4, caption = "Type I error inflation from repeated looks")
```

The empirical false-positive rate is larger than 0.05, confirming that repeated unadjusted testing invalidates the nominal Type I error rate.

## Conclusion

This simulation illustrates the full frequentist workflow for A/B testing:

- estimate the effect with a difference in proportions,
- quantify uncertainty with standard errors and confidence intervals,
- use CLT-based inference for hypothesis testing,
- connect difference-in-means to linear regression,
- and avoid unplanned repeated testing without correction.

Substantively, this sample indicates CTA A has a higher sign-up rate than CTA B. Methodologically, the main lesson is that careful design choices (especially pre-committed stopping rules) are as important as the final p-value.
